steps.title: Track the sonnet contrast
steps.content: The text starts with an apparent contradiction. The sonnet is known for strict formal requirements, so readers might expect sonnets to be rigid or similar to one another. But the examples by Maggie Anderson and Tyehimba Jess differ widely in subject, rhythm, and structure. Nora Ellis then explains why this variety makes sense: when a form's conventions are easy to recognize, poets can use those conventions as material to subvert.

steps.title: Focus on variety
steps.content: Choice A says most modern sonnets are conventional, but the passage emphasizes wide-ranging examples and experimentation. Choice C shifts to whether the sonnet remains popular, a topic the text does not discuss. Choice D invents a long historical period with little innovation, which is also absent. The remaining choice captures the main contrast: although the sonnet's strict characteristics make variety seem unlikely, the form actually encourages surprising experimentation because its recognizable rules invite poets to work against them. The answer is B.
